LAW AND MOTION, 302, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED. CCSF'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS GRANTED AS TO THE NEGLIGENCE CAUSE OF ACTION AND DENIED AS TO THE DANGEROUS CONDITION OF PUBLIC PROPERTY CAUSE OF ACTION. THE COURT GRANTS SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ON THE COMMON LAW NEGLIGENCE CAUSE OF ACTION. CCSF'S LIABILITY IS CONTROLLED BY STATUTE. (SEE GOV'T CODE 815.). ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS). JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR., CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED; DEPT. 302.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Sep-30-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Anna Dubrovsky, Esq. 415-746-1477 x217 for Plaintiff.
David Russo, Esq. 415-362-2580 of Lewis Brisbois for Defendant City and County of San Francisco.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues section GC 835. The Court shares its views. Plaintiff argues the "rat condition"(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant CCSF's motion for summary judgment is denied. CCSF's motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the negligence cause of action and denied as to the dangerous condition of public property cause of action.

To maintain a cause of action under Gov't Code 835 [dangerous condition of public property], plaintiff must show (1) that CCSF owned or maintained the property; (2) that the property was in a dangerous condition on the date of accident; (3) causation; (4) the dangerous condition created a reasonably foreseeable risk of the kind of injury incurred and (5) either CCSF or its employee caused the condition or CCSF had actual or constructive notice of the dangerous condition a sufficient time prior to the time of the accident. (See Gov't Code 830 & 835.)

CCSF argues that it is not liable for plaintiff's meningitis because any exposure to rats occurred in his employer's office and the employer was responsible for pest abatement on those premises. Plaintiff creates a triable issue of material fact whether a dangerous condition existed on public property. Under the CCSF-Sasco lease, CCSF is responsible for the common areas surrounding plaintiff's office. (CCSF's Ex. A (Lease, 2 and 13.8.) Plaintiff's evidence demonstrates that the rats were prevalent in the common areas. (Krepkiy Dec., Exs. H-8, H-18, H-23, H-26; Ex. A (Agurto Depo., 42:7-45:13, 46:11-46:24); Ex. C (Plaintiff's Depo., 85:10-21).)

There is an issue of fact whether the property was in a dangerous condition at the time of plaintiff's illness. In addition, it was reasonably foreseeable that the danger could impose the kind of injury that plaintiff incurred. CCSF's own Integrated Pest Management Guidelines recognize that rats are vectors (carriers) of numerous diseases.

CCSF fails to present evidence showing that it lacked actual or constructive notice of the danger. Indeed, the above-cited evidence shows that CCSF was aware of the rat problem.

CCSF fails to present evidence that the presence of rats did not cause plaintiff's illness. (CCSF's UMF 17-32.) In addition, plaintiff presents competent medical testimony that the rat infestation was a substantial factor in causing plaintiff's meningitis. (Krepkiy, Ex. E (Tolliver Depo. 15:24-24:5, 39:19-40:23, 80:10-24, 114:6-12); Ex. K (medical records); DiGiulo Dec. 1-8.)

The court grants summary adjudication on the common law negligence cause of action. CCSF's liability is controlled by statute. (See Gov't Code 815.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
